meta.title: A208 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes CLK A208 engine — M111, M112, M113. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and CLK 55 AMG V8 explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes CLK A208 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned A208 engine typically costs £1,500–£9,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume M111 four-cylinder petrol sits at £1,500–£3,500, while the M113 V8 in the CLK 55 AMG is the most expensive at £5,000–£9,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on engine complexity and specialist rates. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes CLK A208 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112 3.2-litre V6 (CLK 320, 218 PS) is widely considered the most reliable engine in the A208 range — smooth, torquey, with proven durability and no major systemic failures. The post-facelift M111.982 2.3-litre Kompressor (CLK 230, 197 PS) is also a strong choice, benefiting from the 2000 engine refresh.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes CLK A208?

acceptedAnswer.text: Supercharger clutch failure on the M111 Kompressor engines, cooling system leaks on the M112 V6 and M113 V8, valve stem seal wear on the M113 V8 (particularly the CLK 55 AMG), and biodegradable wiring harness degradation on pre-2000 M111 cars. The 2000 facelift addressed many of these issues.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes CLK A208 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Overall reliability is good for a late-1990s/early-2000s Mercedes. The M111 Kompressor engines are generally robust but require supercharger clutch attention around 60,000–100,000 miles. The M112 V6 is the most reliable option. The main caveat is the pre-2000 biodegradable wiring harness — if it hasn't been replaced, budget for it. Post-2000 cars are much improved.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes CLK A208 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The early M111.944 200 Kompressor (pre-facelift, 192 PS) is the one we'd advise caution on — it suffers from wiring harness issues and the Eaton M62 supercharger can be expensive to repair. The CLK 55 AMG is a genuine modern classic but carries eye-watering maintenance bills — only buy one with deep pockets and full service history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my A208 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Loss of boost or a rattling noise from the front of the engine (supercharger clutch failure on Kompressor cars), persistent coolant loss or overheating (M112/M113 cooling system), blue smoke on startup (valve stem seals on M113), or intermittent misfires and electrical gremlins (wiring harness on pre-2000 M111) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes CLK A208 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000 CLK 320, a £2,500–£4,500 recon is 31–56% of the car's value — viable. Against a tired £3,000 CLK 200, a £1,500–£2,800 recon is 50–93% — borderline. For a CLK 55 AMG, a £30,000 car justifies a £5,000–£9,000 recon; a £15,000 example makes it a much harder call. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes CLK A208 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112 V6 can exceed 200,000 miles with regular servicing and prompt cooling system attention. The M111 Kompressor four-cylinders can reach 150,000–200,000 miles with supercharger clutch replacement around 60,000–100,000 miles. The M113 V8 can last 150,000+ miles but typically requires valve stem seal replacement at 40,000–80,000 miles on AMG variants.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the A208 and C208 CLK?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are the first-generation CLK-Class (1997–2002 for the coupé, 1998–2003 for the cabriolet). The C208 is the 2-door coupé (1997–2002). The A208 is the 2-door cabriolet (1998–2003, this page). Engines are shared across both body styles, though the cabriolet adds structural reinforcement and weight, which slightly affects performance and fuel economy.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes CLK A208 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (under the bonnet or on the driver's door pillar) and on the engine block itself. Tell us your variant (CLK 200, CLK 200 Kompressor, CLK 230 Kompressor, CLK 320, CLK 430, CLK 55 AMG) and approximate model year (pre-2000 or post-2000 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes CLK A208 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first-generation CLK Cabriolet — from the M111 four-cylinder Kompressor to the M112 V6 and the M113 V8 that powers the CLK 55 AMG. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the 2000 engine refresh explained. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes CLK A208 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the A208 cabriolet specifically — including the full M111 Kompressor range, the M112 V6, and the M113 V8 AMG.

overview.intro: The A208 is the cabriolet variant of the first-generation Mercedes-Benz CLK-Class, produced from 1998 to 2003. Based on the W202 C-Class platform, the CLK combined E-Class styling with C-Class underpinnings, creating a new grand touring niche for Mercedes. The engine range spanned M111 four-cylinder petrols — both naturally aspirated and supercharged — plus M112 V6 and M113 V8 units, with a 2000 refresh bringing revised M111 engines and the introduction of the CLK 55 AMG.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W202 (C-Class) • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz E-Class Cabriolet (A124) • Successor: Mercedes-Benz CLK-Class (A209) • Sibling body styles in this generation: C208 coupé (1997–2002).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The A208 CLK Cabriolet generated UK engine enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA], with the M111 Kompressor engines and M112 V6 remaining the most commonly discussed among specialists.

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode — one paragraph: The A208's most dependable engine is the M112 3.2-litre V6 (CLK 320, 218 PS) — smooth, torquey, and widely considered the pick of the range for its refined power delivery and proven durability. The post-facelift M111.982 2.3-litre Kompressor (CLK 230, 197 PS) is the best four-cylinder option — the 2000 refresh addressed many of the earlier M111's issues with a reinforced block and improved cylinder head. The M113 V8 in the CLK 430 (279 PS) offers effortless performance but carries higher running costs, while the CLK 55 AMG (347 PS) is a genuine modern classic — epic V8 soundtrack but eye-watering maintenance bills. Avoid early M111.944 200 Kompressor (192 PS) cars without documented supercharger service history — the Eaton M62 unit can be expensive to rebuild. Also avoid any car with neglected cooling systems, as the M112 and M113 V8s are sensitive to overheating.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Budget Choice — simplest A208 to run
Early Kompressor — more power, more to maintain
Strong Four — 193 PS but early M111 caveats
Most Refined — the V6 sweet spot
V8 Power — effortless but costly to maintain
AMG Icon — epic engine, epic bills

economicsRule.title: The A208 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,500 recon on a sub-£5,000 A208 sits at 50–90% of the car's value — a serious decision. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 31–56% — viable. For a CLK 55 AMG, a £30,000 car justifies a £5,000–£9,000 recon; a £15,000 example makes it a much harder call. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line — value the car first, then the engine.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A208 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CLK 200 (M111.941/945) — simplest engine, cheapest parts, no supercharger.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A208 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CLK 55 AMG (M113.984) — V8 complexity, specialist-only work, eye-watering parts prices.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A208 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: CLK 320 (M112.940) — 218 PS V6, proven reliability, strong performance without AMG running costs.

problems.title: M111 Kompressor Supercharger Clutch Failure
problems.affectedModels: A208 CLK 200 Kompressor, CLK 230 Kompressor (all M111 supercharged variants).
problems.rootCause: The Eaton supercharger's electromagnetic clutch can fail, causing a loss of boost, reduced power, and a rattling noise from the front of the engine. The pre-facelift M62 unit is particularly prone to wear.

tieredCosts.work: Full supercharger replacement if internal damage

problems.recommendation: If your Kompressor is down on power or making a rattling noise from the front of the engine, have the supercharger clutch inspected immediately — £500–£800 is far cheaper than the £1,800+ replacement that follows if the supercharger fails internally. Post-facelift M111.956/M111.982 cars have improved reliability.

cta.label: Compare M111 engine prices for your A208 CLK →

problems.title: M112 & M113 V8 Cooling System Leaks
problems.rootCause: The M112 V6 and M113 V8's cooling systems — particularly the water pump, thermostat housing, and coolant crossover pipes — are prone to leaks. The V6 and V8 layouts make access tight, increasing labour costs.

tieredCosts.work: Replace water pump, thermostat, and coolant pipes
Full cooling system overhaul + engine inspection if overheated

problems.recommendation: Check coolant levels regularly and look for signs of leaks around the water pump and thermostat housing — £500–£900 in preventative work is cheap insurance against the £2,000+ bill that follows an overheat. This is the single most important job on any M112 or M113-powered A208.

cta.label: Compare M112/M113 engine prices for your A208 CLK →

problems.title: M113 V8 Valve Stem Seal Wear (CLK 55 AMG)
problems.rootCause: The M113 V8's valve stem seals can harden and fail over time, leading to oil consumption and blue smoke on startup. This is particularly common on high-performance AMG variants.

tieredCosts.work: Full engine rebuild if oil starvation has caused bottom-end damage

problems.recommendation: If your CLK 55 is smoking on startup or using oil between services, have the valve stem seals inspected immediately — a £2,000–£3,500 repair is far cheaper than the £5,000+ rebuild that follows. Budget for this as a preventative job at 50,000 miles if you plan to keep the car long-term.

cta.label: Compare M113 engine prices for your A208 CLK 55 AMG →

problems.title: Pre-2000 M111 Wiring Harness Degradation
problems.affectedModels: A208 CLK 200, CLK 200 Kompressor, CLK 230 Kompressor (pre-facelift, 1998–2000).
problems.rootCause: Early A208s used biodegradable wiring insulation that degrades with age and heat, causing short circuits, misfires, and erratic engine behaviour — the same issue that affects the W202 C-Class.
problems.recommendation: If your pre-facelift A208 has no documented wiring harness replacement, budget for it immediately — £400–£700 is cheap insurance against the £1,000+ bill for chasing intermittent electrical faults. Post-facelift cars (2000–2002) are much improved.

cta.label: Compare M111 wiring harness replacement quotes for your A208 CLK →

replacementCosts.h2: A208 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are A208 cabriolet only. C208 coupé shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since body style affects weight, aerodynamics, and to some extent, labour access. Production of the A208 cabriolet continued until March 2003, one year longer than the coupé.
